name: Connect Bay Area regional transportation sales tax
description: State-authorized five-county transportation sales tax proposed for the November 2026 ballot; San Francisco share would be dedicated to transit stabilization.
legal_eligibility: Yes for transit operations if approved; official SFCTA materials say Muni's share would address the structural deficit.
estimated_amount: Approx. $155 million/year for Muni if passed
vote_threshold: Simple majority at regional ballot (per current measure design)
precedent: Regional dedicated transit taxes are common, but this exact multi-county structure is pending.
dependencies: Requires ballot qualification, voter approval, and successful regional coalition
notes: Strongest identified recurring source in current official strategy. Source: https://www.sfcta.org/blogs/updates-regional-transportation-revenue-measure-and-muni-parcel-tax

name: San Francisco Muni parcel tax
description: Local parcel tax designed to complement the regional measure and reduce Muni's operating deficit while funding some service improvements.
legal_eligibility: Yes if structured and approved as proposed; intended specifically for Muni stabilization and improvements.
estimated_amount: Approx. $150 million/year for deficit reduction plus approx. $10 million/year for service improvements
vote_threshold: Likely two-thirds if treated as a special tax
precedent: Would be new, but parcel taxes are common local revenue tools.
dependencies: Needs local coalition, ballot placement, and voter approval
notes: Powerful but politically exposed because property owners will organize around tax design. Source: https://www.sfcta.org/blogs/updates-regional-transportation-revenue-measure-and-muni-parcel-tax

name: Agency efficiencies and new enterprise revenue
description: Internal savings, business-process changes, parking/pricing optimization, and other enterprise revenues used to narrow the deficit.
legal_eligibility: Yes for agency budget balancing; already part of SFMTA's official plan.
estimated_amount: Not fully specified; official materials treat it as a meaningful but not sufficient share of gap closure
precedent: Already underway inside SFMTA's budget planning.
dependencies: Management execution, sometimes Board approval, and sometimes public acceptance of fee changes
notes: Best framed as necessary self-help, not a complete solution. Sources: https://www.sfmta.com/projects/sfmta-budget-planning-fy-2026-27-and-fy-2027-28 ; https://www.sfcta.org/blogs/updates-regional-transportation-revenue-measure-and-muni-parcel-tax

name: General Fund stabilization transfer
description: City general-fund support or backfill to protect essential transit access and avoid deeper service cuts.
legal_eligibility: Yes within city budget process, but competes with other priorities and charter constraints.
estimated_amount: Flexible; amount depends on city fiscal capacity and political prioritization
vote_threshold: Annual budget adoption; subject to mayoral and Board politics
precedent: City general-fund support is part of SFMTA's broader fiscal structure.
dependencies: Mayor support, Board support, overall city budget health
notes: Useful as bridge or match, but politically vulnerable because opponents can argue it crowds out other services.

name: State Transit Assistance (STA) block-grant programming
description: Flexible state transit funding distributed through SFCTA/MTC that can support lifeline transit operating and capital needs.
legal_eligibility: Yes; official SFCTA page says STA can be used for a wide range of operating and capital purposes.
estimated_amount: Variable/volatile; annual allocation depends on diesel-sales-tax revenues
vote_threshold: Programmatic allocation rather than public vote
precedent: Existing Bay Area transit funding source
dependencies: Annual allocation levels and SFCTA/MTC programming choices
notes: More realistic for targeted pilots or partial fare-free expansion than for replacing the full farebox. Source: https://www.sfcta.org/funding/state-transit-assistance-county-block-grant

name: Low Carbon Transit Operations Program (LCTOP)
description: California climate-linked transit program that can fund operating or capital assistance, prioritizing disadvantaged communities and emissions reduction.
legal_eligibility: Yes for qualifying service expansions and operations that reduce greenhouse-gas emissions and improve mobility.
estimated_amount: Variable competitive/formula funding; not large enough by itself for full system replacement
precedent: Used statewide for transit operations, service, and fare-related projects
dependencies: Project eligibility, annual appropriations, and compliance with climate-benefit rules
notes: Best for targeted fare-free pilots or disadvantaged-community expansions, not as the lone long-term revenue source. Source: https://dot.ca.gov/programs/rail/low-carbon-transit-operations-program-lctop

name: Employer institutional pass partnerships
description: Large-employer or institutional agreements that subsidize rider access through bulk purchasing, discounted Muni tickets, or commuter-benefit structures.
legal_eligibility: Yes; already familiar through SFMTA commute-support and employer commuter-benefit structures.
estimated_amount: Moderate and negotiated; unlikely to replace full farebox alone
vote_threshold: No public vote; requires contract/program design
precedent: Employer commuter benefits and bulk-ticket programs already exist in San Francisco.
dependencies: Employer participation, contract design, and labor market conditions
notes: Good stack component for targeted worker access or neighborhood partnerships, weak as sole citywide replacement source. Sources: https://www.sfmta.com/commute-support-program ; https://www.sfenvironment.org/commuter-benefits-ordinance-sf

name: Emergency bridge loan / one-time rescue financing
description: Short-term external financing like the 2026 Bay Area transit loan used to avoid near-term service collapse while longer-term measures are pursued.
legal_eligibility: Yes for short-term stabilization, but it is debt/bridge support rather than permanent operating revenue.
estimated_amount: Context-specific; 2026 state package was $590 million regionwide, not Muni-only
dependencies: State willingness, repayment plan, and broader regional negotiations
notes: Important for timeline/viability, but should never be described as a solution to permanent fare-free operations. Sources: https://www.gov.ca.gov/2026/02/19/governor-newsom-signs-legislation-authorizing-590-million-emergency-loan-to-bay-area-transit/ ; https://mtc.ca.gov/news/governor-signs-bill-authorizing-loan-bay-area-transit-agencies
